metadata.summary: Fully-populated shopping cart spec covering problem statement, personas, user stories, FR/NFR, acceptance criteria, out-of-scope, dependencies, and data model. Use as few-shot reference.

content.title: Spec Example: E-commerce Cart
content.summary: A fully-populated SDD spec for a shopping cart feature — covering personas, user stories, functional and non-functional requirements, Given/When/Then acceptance criteria, out-of-scope table, and a preliminary data model. Use as a few-shot reference when generating or reviewing specs for similar features.

why.p: Agents writing specs without a concrete reference produce structurally incomplete documents: missing the "Out of Scope / When" column, omitting open questions, copying story-point estimates instead of token budgets, and writing NFRs without measurement sources. A worked example with these sections present (and some intentionally incomplete) teaches by demonstration what a complete spec looks like and where gaps hide.

list.item: Agent or developer needs to see what a fully-populated spec looks like in practice.
Calibrating spec-writing output quality — compare agent output against this example.
Bootstrapping a new e-commerce cart-adjacent feature (persistence, guest-vs-logged-in, quantity limits, session merge patterns recur across cart-related features).
Onboarding a new contributor to the SDD workflow.
Starting a new spec by editing this file directly — use template-spec instead.
Non-e-commerce domains: guest cart, localStorage, product-catalog dependency are domain-specific and transfer poorly without significant reframing.
Taking NFR targets (200ms p95, 50k concurrent) as real project targets — they have no measurement source and must be replaced.

section.name: problem-statement
section.p: WHO: Online shoppers on an e-commerce platform. PROBLEM: Cannot save products for later purchase or manage multiple items before checkout. IMPACT: Users abandon purchases due to friction, reducing conversion rate from 2.1% baseline. SOLUTION: Persistent shopping cart with quantity management and price calculation. SUCCESS METRIC: Increase conversion rate from 2.1% to 3.5%.

example.title: Persona 1: Busy Parent Sarah
example.description: Role: Working parent shopping for household items. Goal: Add items throughout the week and checkout once. Pain: Forgets items, no time for multiple checkout sessions. Context: Mobile during commute, desktop at home.

example.title: Persona 2: Deal Hunter Mike
example.description: Role: Budget-conscious shopper comparing prices. Goal: Save items, wait for sales, then purchase. Pain: Items disappear from browser, can't track prices. Context: Multiple devices, checks daily.

rule.statement: Define at least two personas with distinct goals and pain points; single-persona specs miss edge cases.
rule.rationale: Sarah drives the "quick add" UX; Mike drives the "persistence across sessions" requirement. Different personas justify different acceptance criteria that would otherwise look redundant.

section.name: user-stories-and-functional-requirements
section.p: US-001: As Sarah, I want to add products with one click so I can save items for later checkout. Priority: Must. Traces to: AC-001, AC-002. US-002: As a shopper, I want to change quantities so I can adjust my order. Priority: Must. Traces to: AC-003. US-003: As a shopper, I want to remove products. Priority: Must. Traces to: AC-004. US-004: As Mike, I want my cart to persist across sessions. Priority: Must. Traces to: AC-005, AC-006.

list.item: FR-001: System SHALL allow adding products to cart (US-001). Validation: product must be in stock; duplicate adds increase quantity; max 99 per product.
FR-002: System SHALL update cart total in real-time (US-001).
FR-003: System SHALL allow quantity updates 1-99 (US-002).
FR-004: System SHALL allow removing items (US-003).
FR-005: System SHALL persist cart for logged-in users (US-004).
FR-006: System SHALL use localStorage for guest users, expire after 7 days (US-004).
NFR-001 Performance: Add-to-cart operation < 200ms p95 client-side. Validation: load test with 50k concurrent users. [SOURCE REQUIRED — replace with real measurement]
NFR-002 Scalability: Support 50k concurrent cart operations. [SOURCE REQUIRED]
NFR-003 Availability: Cart data recovery RTO < 1 min.
NFR-004 Usability: Cart visible in 1 click from any page.

rule.statement: Mark every NFR target with [SOURCE REQUIRED] when adapting this example; never copy numeric targets without a real measurement basis.
rule.rationale: NFR values without measurement sources survive unchanged into design and implementation. The 200ms / 50k figures are plausible for a mid-scale platform but have no basis in any specific project.

section.name: acceptance-criteria

example.title: AC-001 Happy Path
example.description: Given product "Wireless Mouse $25" in stock and empty cart. When user clicks "Add to Cart". Then: product added, badge shows "1", total shows "$25.00", success message 2s, product page stays.

example.title: AC-002 Duplicate
example.description: Given cart has "Wireless Mouse" qty 1. When user clicks "Add to Cart" again. Then: qty becomes 2, total "$50.00", no duplicate entry.

example.title: AC-003 Quantity Update
example.description: Given cart has "Wireless Mouse" qty 2. When user changes qty to 5. Then: total "$125.00", DB/localStorage updates within 500ms.

example.title: AC-004 Remove
example.description: Given cart has 2 products (total $75). When user removes "Wireless Mouse $25". Then: product gone, total "$50.00", badge updates.

example.title: AC-005 Logged-in Persistence
example.description: Given logged-in user with "Wireless Mouse" in cart. When browser closed and reopened 2 days later after login. Then: cart still contains item with current price.

example.title: AC-006 Guest Persistence
example.description: Given guest user with "Wireless Mouse". When browser closed and returned within 7 days. Then: cart still contains item.

section.p: Coverage matrix: [x] Happy path [x] Duplicate adds [x] Quantity limits [x] Persistence logged-in [x] Guest persistence [ ] Security (cart tampering) — documented risk [ ] Performance (large carts) — documented risk

list.item: Save for Later — Phase 2
Share Cart via URL — Phase 3 (complexity)
Cart Price Alerts — Not planned (external dependency)
Multi-currency — v2.0 (international expansion only)

rule.statement: Include a "When" column in Out of Scope; "Not now" without a timeline reappears as scope creep in the next sprint.
rule.rationale: The "Out of Scope / When" column is the highest-ROI section for preventing feature creep mid-sprint. Copy this pattern into every spec.

list.item: Constraint: Max 50 unique products per cart (DB performance)
Constraint: Max 99 quantity per product (business rule)
Constraint: localStorage max 5MB (browser limit)
Internal dep: Product Catalog (feature 01) — must be done
Internal dep: User Authentication (feature 02) — for logged-in cart
External dep: PostgreSQL database
External dep: Redis for cart session caching
Blocks: Checkout (feature 04)

rule.statement: Mark all data models in a spec as "preliminary — subject to change in design.md".
rule.rationale: Agents treat TypeScript interfaces in spec Appendix as canonical data models. The spec model is intent only; the approved model lives in design.md after architectural decisions are made.

example.title: Preliminary Cart Data Model

list.item: Should cart merge when guest user logs in? (cart merge on login is the most commonly overlooked question in cart specs)
How to handle out-of-stock items already in cart?
Show "X people have this in cart" social proof?

rule.statement: Include an Open Questions section in every spec; if there are no questions, either the spec is complete or the author didn't look hard enough.
rule.rationale: Open questions are forcing functions: unanswered, they become assumptions in design.md. Documented, they become explicit review targets before approval.

list.item: FR → US traceability table: every FR row has a Traces To column pointing to US-X
NFR measurement format: requirement + target + measurement method + validation load test
AC Given/When/Then with specific values: "$25.00" not "valid price", "2 days later" not "some time"
Out of Scope with "When" column: Phase 2 / Phase 3 / v2.0 / Not planned
AC coverage matrix with explicit unchecked items as documented risks
Open Questions as a forcing function — minimum 3 per complex feature

example.title: Few-shot generation prompt
example.description: Use the following completed spec as a reference for structure and quality. Reference spec: [paste content/01-full-spec.xml]. Now write a spec for: [new feature name]. Problem: [problem statement]. User types: [list]. Key constraints: [list]. Follow the same section structure. Replace all domain-specific content. NFRs must use [MEASURE_NEEDED] where targets are unknown.

example.title: Gap analysis prompt
example.description: Compare this draft spec against the e-commerce cart example. Identify: (1) sections present in example but missing from draft, (2) AC scenarios (error, edge case, persistence) missing from draft, (3) traceability gaps (FR with no AC, US with no FR). Output: gap list only.

antipattern.description: Agent copies story-point estimates (5 pts, 3 pts) from this example into new specs.
antipattern.reason: SDD replaces story points with token estimates. Explicitly instruct agents to remove story points and replace with token estimates per the 100k rule.

antipattern.description: Agent treats the TypeScript interface in the Appendix as the canonical data model.
antipattern.reason: The interface is labeled preliminary. Without this label, agents use it in design.md as approved, bypassing the architectural decision process.

antipattern.description: Agent copies NFR targets (200ms p95, 50k concurrent) verbatim into new specs.
antipattern.reason: These values have no measurement source. Add a required [SOURCE] tag next to each NFR target when adapting to force replacement with real measurements.

antipattern.description: Agent expands personas (Busy Parent Sarah, Deal Hunter Mike) with hallucinated pain points.
antipattern.reason: Persona pain points must come from real user research. Agents generate plausible-sounding but fabricated persona context. Provide real research or use placeholder text explicitly.

script.purpose: Print all unchecked AC coverage items from a spec file
